title: Cigar Case
description: From Card: "Flat, round contoured tortoise shell case made of one part fitting closely over the other, painted on outside design of a flower and a bird in gold and red. Acquired in 1882 in San Francisco."

title: Harvey Milk, 18th & Castro Streets, San Francisco
description: Born Woodmere, Long Island, New York

title: Flyer promoting the Citizenship Education Project
description: This document is part of a scrapbook that was compiled in 1956 and 1957 by Frances Albrier during her term as president of the San Francisco Chapter of the National Council of Negro Women (NCNW). The scrapbook highlights the Chapter’s efforts to register voters and educate Bay Area residents on the importance of voting as a part of the Citizenship Education Project which was jointly sponsored by the NCNW and the National Urban League.

title: Shangri La
description: Length of woven fabric with a foundation of beige and copper metallic. Broad vertical stripes are broken up by rectangles of long, off-white uncut loops.

title: PLUCK 'EM
architect: Rachael Romero, Australian, active USA, b. 1953

title: San Francisco Giants spring training jersey worn and signed by Willie Mays
description: A short-sleeved beige baseball jersey. There are seven (7) clear plastic buttons going down the center front of the jersey on the proper right side and the buttonholes are on the proper left side. The jersey reads [GIANTS] across the front with the "GIA" on the proper right side and the "NTS" on the proper left side. The "G" and the "S" have a series of curves and pints around them. The letters are black patches with orange outlines.

title: Photographic print of Eunice Jackson and another woman wearing leis
description: A black-and-white photograph of Eunice Jackson and another woman wearing leis. The women are wearing hats, jackets, and dresses, and are holding their glasses in their right hands. They are carrying other items under their left arms. Jackson is wearing a fur coat over her outfit, and the other woman is holding a fur coat or stole in her left hand. In the background on the proper left, a florist's stand is visible, and on the proper right there is an elevator. On the verso of the photograph, handwriting identifies the second woman as "Dot." The left and right sides of the photograph are scalloped.
